critic_raw:
Issue 1 — Prediction vs. Intervention Gap
The proposal conflates two distinct claims: (a) the model can predict which subscribers are likely to churn, and (b) sending discounts to predicted churners will reduce churn. The evidence supports (a): AUROC 0.81 on retrospective data. It does not support (b). The proposal's logic chain is: predict churn → send discount → churn prevented. But this requires that discounts cause retention, which is not established. The observation that historical discount recipients had lower observed churn is an observational correlation, not causal evidence. Customers who received discounts from agents were selected because agents judged them likely to respond positively — this is confounding by indication. A customer who receives a discount because they complained is different from a model-selected customer who has not complained. Evidence needed: a randomized controlled trial assigning discounts to model-selected high-risk subscribers and measuring churn vs. a holdout control group.

Issue 2 — Selection Bias in Historical Offers
The historical discount data is not a random sample of high-risk customers receiving treatment. Discounts were given for multiple reasons: complaint resolution, competitive threats, retention campaigns. This creates selection bias: the 'treated' group in the historical data is not the same population as the model's top-decile output will be. Customers who received discounts from agents may be systematically different (more vocal, more likely to respond to intervention, facing competitor pricing) from the silent churners the model identifies. Using the observed discount-recipient churn rate to argue that future model-directed discounts will work confounds treatment assignment mechanism with treatment effect. Evidence needed: comparison of churn rate among high-risk users who did NOT receive discounts (untreated controls) vs. low-risk users; or an intent-to-treat analysis from a pilot.
defender_raw:
Pass 1 — Analysis:
Issue 1: The prediction-intervention gap is a valid and important concern. The proposal treats AUROC as evidence of intervention efficacy. It is not. The observational correlation between historical discount receipt and lower churn is explicitly acknowledged as not from a randomized experiment and as stemming from multiple reasons for discount assignment. The defender can argue that the model is a reasonable starting point for a pilot, but cannot argue the causal claim is established.

Issue 2: The selection-in-historical-offers concern is also valid. Historical discounts are not a random sample of model-equivalent treatment. The argument that discount recipients had lower churn does not control for the selection process by which those customers received discounts.

Pass 2 — Verdicts:
Issue 1: Concede — the proposal does not establish that intervention will work, only that prediction is good.
Issue 2: Concede — selection bias in historical offers makes the causal inference invalid.
Overall: critique_wins.
adjudication_raw:
ADJUDICATION — scope_intent_abandonment_offer_301 — Isolated Debate Run 1

Both issues conceded. The core problem is a scope jump: from prediction capability (AUROC 0.81) to intervention efficacy (discounts will reduce churn). The historical discount data is confounded by selection. Without a randomized experiment, the causal claim is not established.

Verdict: CRITIQUE_WINS
